figures.caption: Figure 1. Overview of the CaloArt backbone. To improve parameter efficiency, all transformer blocks share the same adaLN-single modulation branch. The function g(Ei, β̄, γ̄) denotes the block-specific combination of the shared shift and scale with the trainable embedding Ei of the i-th transformer block. The resulting shift and scale parameters are used by adaptive normalization in that block.

Figure 2. FPD decreases as transformer backbone Gflops increase on CCD2. Each point shows one of our seven CCD2 DiT models, using its backbone Gflops and the lowest FPD reached within 400K training steps. The trend follows the DiT compute–quality relation: higher backbone compute, from either scaling the backbone or using finer patches, gives better sample quality.

Figure 8. Classifier-based Gflops-scaling plots on CCD2. Left: cls-low AUC versus transformer Gflops. Middle: cls-high AUC versus transformer Gflops. Right: cls-resnet AUC versus transformer Gflops. These figures provide classifier-based counterparts to the FPD-versus-Gflops result shown in the main text. For the cls-resnet AUC panel, the visibly anomalous h256-l6 | (3,8,5) point is excluded from the fit-oriented visualization.

Table 8. Summary of published CCD3 (ds3) submissions on common evaluation metrics. Classifier AUCs are taken from Table C23, FPD from Table C24, and timing reports GPU generation time per shower at batch size 100 from Table C30. Geant4 timing is not reported in the source table.

Figure 4. FPD–generation-time Pareto view on CCD3. CCD3 methods with reported GPU generation time are compared using time per shower and FPD ×103, with lower-left preferred. Blue points mark published Pareto-front submissions, and the red star marks CaloArt, which remains Pareto-efficient in this comparison.

Figure 6. Histograms of high-level features for the CCD2 detector. From left to right and top to bottom: center of energy in the eta and phi directions, sparsity, width of the center of energy in the eta and phi directions, weighted depth by slice, layer energy depositions, total deposited energy ratio Etot/Einc, and voxel energy distribution.
Table 1. Comparison on CCD2 between some published transformer backbone models and our CaloArt result. Geant4 is shown as a reference.

Figure 3. Relative change from v-prediction to x-prediction in three representative configurations. Each panel reports FPD and three classifier AUC metrics. For FPD, the value is (xpred − vpred)/vpred; for AUCs, it is the relative change in distance from 0.5, measuring classifier distinguishability. Negative values favor x-prediction, while positive values favor v-prediction. Panels (a) and (b) show two CCD2 patch-size settings with mixed metric-level effects, whereas panel (c) shows a representative CCD3 setting where x-prediction improves all metrics.

Figure 7. Histograms of high-level features for the CCD3 detector. From left to right and top to bottom: center of energy in the eta and phi directions, sparsity, width of the center of energy in the eta and phi directions, weighted depth by slice, layer energy depositions, total deposited energy ratio Etot/Einc, and voxel energy distribution.

Figure 5. Validation-loss comparison between x-prediction and v-prediction on CCD3. Both curves are shown against global_step for the same setting. The callouts report the FPD values at the 50K- and 100K-step checkpoints for both formulations.

Table 2. Model scale, tokenization, and sampling configurations for representative transformerbased diffusion and flow calorimeter generators. Reported Gflops follow the DiT-compatible forward-pass convention discussed above.
Table 6. Configurations of the final CaloArt models. The table reports the training configurations of the two final CCD2 and CCD3 models used for the main results.
